era_context: The late 15th century saw the consolidation of early modern European nation-states, ongoing Ottoman expansion, Ming Dynasty stability, and the final stages of the Reconquista. This was a period of dynastic succession, religious conflict, and the early Age of Exploration.

events.title: Pazzi Conspiracy in Florence
events.description: The Pazzi family, rivals of the Medici, orchestrated an assassination attempt against Lorenzo de' Medici and his brother Giuliano during Easter Mass at the Florence Cathedral on April 26, 1478. Giuliano was killed, but Lorenzo escaped. The conspiracy, backed by Pope Sixtus IV and King Ferdinand of Naples, aimed to overthrow Medici dominance. The plot failed catastrophically for the conspirators; the Pazzi were hunted down and executed, and the Medici emerged with consolidated power. This event marked a turning point in Florentine politics and demonstrated the Medici's resilience and popular support.

sources.name: Florentine state records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: The date (April 26, 1478) is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources. The event is one of the most thoroughly attested conspiracies of the Renaissance.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War Continues
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued with Ottoman forces pressing advantages in the Aegean and Adriatic. In 1478, Ottoman forces under Mehmed II maintained pressure on Venetian holdings, particularly in the Peloponnese and Aegean islands. Venice, economically strained and militarily outmatched, was moving toward negotiation. This year saw continued Ottoman naval dominance and Venetian territorial losses.
events.certainty_note: The war's continuation in 1478 is well-documented. Specific military engagements are recorded in both Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Spanish Inquisition Established
events.description: The Spanish Inquisition was formally established in 1478 by papal bull (Sixtus IV) at the request of Isabella and Ferdinand. The institution was designed to investigate heresy, particularly among converted Jews (conversos) and Muslims (moriscos). The Inquisition became one of the most powerful and feared institutions in Spain, operating with royal support and ecclesiastical authority. Its establishment marked a significant escalation in religious persecution and would profoundly shape Spanish society for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The establishment date of 1478 is well-documented in papal and royal records. The formal institution began operations in 1480.

events.title: Siena Under Medici Influence
events.description: Following the Pazzi Conspiracy, Medici influence in Tuscany expanded. Siena, a rival republic to Florence, came increasingly under Medici pressure and influence. This period saw the gradual erosion of Siena's independence, a process that would culminate in Medici domination and eventual incorporation into the Grand Duchy of Tuscany.
events.certainty_note: The general trend of Medici expansion is well-documented; specific events in Siena for 1478 are less prominent in surviving records.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of African Coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator (who died in 1460, but whose legacy continued), continued systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1478, Portuguese traders had established forts and trading posts along the Gold Coast (modern Ghana). The Portuguese were developing a slave trade and acquiring gold, ivory, and other commodities. This exploration laid the groundwork for the later Portuguese maritime empire.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and merchant accounts
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented; specific events for 1478 are less detailed in surviving records.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The Pazzi Conspiracy is sometimes portrayed as a purely internal Florentine affair, but modern scholarship emphasizes the involvement of Pope Sixtus IV and King Ferdinand of Naples, making it a broader Italian political event. The Spanish Inquisition's establishment date of 1478 is sometimes conflated with its actual operational beginning in 1480; the institution was formally authorized in 1478 but did not begin investigations until 1480. Portuguese exploration is sometimes overstated as a coordinated 'Age of Exploration' in 1478; it was actually a gradual, commercially-driven process with no single organizing principle.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1478 is rich in Western European sources, particularly Italian and Iberian. Ottoman sources are detailed for military campaigns. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are available but focus on court politics. African, American, and Asian sources beyond China are sparse or absent from Western historiography. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe; economic and social change in Africa and Asia are underrepresented. The Pazzi Conspiracy is exceptionally well-documented due to its dramatic nature and impact on Renaissance Florence; this may create a distorted sense of its historical significance relative to other events.

graph_edges.note: Pazzi Conspiracy led to consolidation of Medici power in 1479
Continued Ottoman pressure led to Treaty of Constantinople in 1479
Spanish Inquisition's establishment led to expulsion of Jews in 1492
Portuguese exploration of African coast led to rounding of Cape of Good Hope
